title: Balance
summary: Distribution of visual weight across a composition — symmetrical for calm and stability, asymmetrical for energy with control.
how: Use symmetrical balance for finance, healthcare, settings, and admin surfaces.
Use asymmetrical balance for landing pages, editorial, and creative brand work.
Balance density, not only geometry — a saturated red card outweighs a larger gray panel.
implementation: One heavy focal area per viewport is usually enough.
If the left side has dense text, counterweight with image mass, a stronger CTA block, or more spacing on the right.
On dashboards, avoid stacking every KPI and alert into the top-left corner.
examples: Apple product pages: large product visual opposite short controlled copy and a clear CTA.
Symmetrical: headers, pricing tables, settings panels.
failure_mode: Too much dense content in one quadrant makes the UI feel unstable. Empty dead zones combined with overloaded corners look unfinished.

title: Contrast
summary: Difference in light versus dark, large versus small, thick versus thin — creates distinction, readability, and focus.
how: Build contrast with color, size, weight, spacing, and shape.
Reserve your strongest contrast for the most important action or message.
Use low contrast for supportive text, never for critical text.
implementation: Body text should meet at least 4.5:1 contrast (WCAG AA).
Large headings can go lower, but AA compliance is the safer floor for production.
Primary and secondary buttons must not rely only on label wording to differentiate intent.
examples: Nike: bold black-and-white with a single urgent accent, product and headline dominate instantly.
Spotify: high-contrast surfaces with vivid green reserved for action and brand recall.
failure_mode: Many high-contrast colors at once destroy hierarchy. Low-contrast labels on tinted cards make dashboards unreadable despite looking polished.

title: Emphasis and Hierarchy
summary: Emphasis identifies the focal point; hierarchy ranks everything else beneath it. If everything shouts, the interface becomes silent.
how: Decide the first thing the user should notice in under 2 seconds.
Support it with second-level context, then tertiary details.
Keep hierarchy to three clear levels on a single screen whenever possible.
implementation: Use a size jump of at least 1.3× to 1.5× between hierarchy levels.
Give the primary action the strongest contrast, strongest label, and clearest position.
On dense screens, emphasis can come from whitespace as much as from color.
examples: Stripe: one sentence, one product visual, one CTA — then proof and detail.
Meta: content creation and feed engagement emphasized; secondary settings stay visually quiet.
failure_mode: Multiple primary CTAs create indecision. Making every number, card title, and badge bold removes rank entirely.

title: Placement and Proximity
summary: The eye assumes nearby items are related. Actions should live next to the objects they control.
how: Place actions near the object they control.
Put labels, helper text, and validation near the field they describe.
Group related content before adding visual dividers.
implementation: Primary actions belong at the end of the reading path or directly adjacent to the object they affect.
Top-left and center-left remain high-value areas for scanning.
For pricing tables, keep plan name, price, main benefit, and CTA tightly grouped.
examples: Amazon: product title, rating, price, delivery promise, and purchase action in one tight decision cluster.
AJIO: icon plus label reduces ambiguity and increases tap target area in navigation.
failure_mode: Global actions too far from affected objects increase friction. Wide gaps between label and field create comprehension lag.

title: Alignment
summary: Invisible structure that makes interfaces feel intentional. Aligned elements reduce visual noise and support scanning.
how: Align text to a consistent edge.
Use shared baselines and card widths.
Keep input fields, labels, and controls lined up within a form.
implementation: Prefer left-aligned body text for interfaces using Latin scripts.
Use one dominant column edge per section.
Avoid floating objects that ignore grid logic unless that break is deliberate and rare.
examples: Linear's interface feels premium partly because alignment is extremely strict across type, panels, list items, and controls.
failure_mode: Near-alignment is worse than obvious misalignment because it looks accidental — the eye notices the imprecision without understanding why.

title: Movement and Rhythm
summary: How the eye travels across a layout. Repeated intervals make that movement feel predictable and guide the user through narrative beats.
how: Build flows from headline to explanation to proof to action.
Use repeated card heights, margins, and content blocks to create scroll rhythm.
Use animation to confirm action, not to perform theater.
implementation: Keep entrance animations short — 150 to 300 ms.
Use stagger only when it supports comprehension.
For onboarding, number steps or use a visible progress system.
examples: Apple launch pages: controlled scroll choreography moves the eye through narrative beats.
Notion: spatial progression and progressive disclosure lets users discover features without overwhelming the first screen.
failure_mode: Animated backgrounds, autoplay motion, or attention-grabbing loaders overwhelm the core task and signal low confidence.

title: White Space
summary: Deliberate absence of clutter. Creates focus, separation, elegance, and breathing room — especially important for trust-heavy and premium surfaces.
how: Use it to define groups before adding borders.
Increase space around primary actions and major sections.
Use dense spacing only when the audience benefits from density and already understands the system.
implementation: Premium and trust-heavy brands often benefit from more white space.
Internal tools can be denser but should still preserve clear grouping with consistent spacing tokens.
Section spacing: 48–96 px on desktop, 24–40 px on mobile between major sections.
examples: Apple and luxury commerce: white space signals confidence and premium value.
Stripe's documentation: generous space makes dense technical content scannable.
failure_mode: Too little space feels noisy. Too much space without hierarchy feels empty and under-designed.

title: Proportion and Scale
summary: Scale signals importance before language does. The relationship between sizes creates visual rank and emotional weight.
how: Make the most important object obviously larger.
Use modular type and spacing scales.
Adjust scale by device, not by percentage alone.
implementation: A dashboard KPI should be larger than surrounding labels by at least one clear step.
Hero headlines can be oversized, but supporting text must remain readable beside them.
Use a type scale with at least 1.25× ratio between adjacent levels.
examples: Nike: oversized typography and dominant photography create energy and urgency.
Bloomberg: controlled scale highlights key numbers without making the whole screen noisy.
failure_mode: Huge logos, oversized icons, or tiny body copy create amateur proportion immediately. Everything large means nothing is large.

title: Pattern and Repetition
summary: Consistent visual and interaction patterns lower interpretation cost. Users stop re-learning the interface with each screen.
how: Keep cards, list rows, nav sections, and tables structurally consistent.
Repeat type scale, icon style, radius, elevation logic, and spacing rules.
Repeat interaction states: hover, press, focus, success, and error.
implementation: Define reusable components, not just reusable colors.
Audit whether a component still works at mobile and dense desktop states.
If two buttons perform equivalent roles, they should look equivalent.
examples: Airbnb search results: repeating card patterns help the brain compare listings quickly.
Meta: repeated icon treatments, chip styles, avatars, and modal patterns make large systems learnable.
failure_mode: Blind repetition without context makes products feel generic. Decorative patterns behind text reduce readability and feel dated.

title: Unity
summary: The sense that the whole interface belongs to one system. Unity creates trust — disconnected styles look improvised and unreliable.
how: Standardize palette, component logic, type pairings, radius, spacing, and interaction tone.
Let one design language run across marketing, app, support, and settings.
Use design tokens for color, type, spacing, and motion.
implementation: Keep illustration style, icon stroke, and border logic coherent.
One component that breaks pattern should document the reason.
Limit typeface families to two: one for display, one for body.
examples: Apple: consistent materials, type, spacing, and motion language across product, marketing, and OS surfaces.
Stripe: code-forward visuals, strong hierarchy, and disciplined spacing feel coherent across docs, dashboard, and marketing.
failure_mode: Mixing three button styles, two card philosophies, and unrelated type systems kills credibility instantly.

title: Variety
summary: Controlled difference that prevents the interface from becoming flat or predictable. Unity without variety is sterile; variety without unity is chaos.
how: Vary scale, imagery, section backgrounds, or component arrangement while keeping the underlying system consistent.
Use variety at the section level more often than at the component level.
Let one accent, one hero visual, or one alternate card treatment provide lift.
implementation: Introduce variety through full-bleed sections, alternating image-text layouts, or a distinct hero treatment.
Keep variety to 20% of the design; repetition should dominate.
examples: Spotify: variety in album art, gradients, and playlist imagery while the system beneath remains highly repetitive.
failure_mode: Too much novelty in every section makes the brand feel unstable and exhausting to read.

title: Designed Constraints and Ethics
summary: Digital products encode permission, restriction, sequence, and power into the object itself — not just presentation but behavior.
how: Ask: what are we helping the user do? And: what are we silently forcing, preventing, or biasing?
Treat defaults, required paths, and disabled states as policy decisions, not just visual choices.
Distinguish good constraints (validation, safety, privacy) from dark patterns (hidden cancellation, obscured export, forced upgrades).
implementation: Good: disable submit until required fields are valid; warn before destructive actions; rate limits; privacy permissions.
Avoid: obscuring cancellation; hiding export behind confusing flows; countdown timer dark patterns; making comparison impossible.
Run the ethics check: would this flow still feel fair if the user were in a hurry or under stress?
examples: Shopify: hides logistics complexity behind guided flows and prebuilt templates — complexity moved to where users are ready for it.
LinkedIn profile completion: uses Zeigarnik Effect honestly — shows remaining steps to motivate, not to trap.
failure_mode: Good UX that converts well by hiding alternatives may be effective but is not good design. Interface-encoded manipulation is still manipulation.
